title: Nova Locutio Claim Expression
description: Structured AST for claims made by the `assert` speech act. The v0.1 message schema's `assert.claim` is a string in surface syntax; this schema defines the structured form that v0.2+ of the message schema will require. Kinds: `predicate` (wraps an arbitrary predicate-expression), `observed` (a predicate conditioned on a recorded effect trace — the effectful counterpart of `predicate`: any receiver replays the computation against the trace, no effect grants or secrets needed, and confirms the result follows deterministically from the recorded observations; the observations themselves remain the signer's testimony, priced by the trust model), `satisfies` (subject satisfies a named property — the common case, sugar for a `predicate` with the equivalent `app('satisfies', subject, property)` shape), `verified` (subject artifact has been verified by some agent, optionally with a proof certificate), `attestation` (a trust statement *about an agent or artifact* — the building block of the trust graph; see trust-model.md), and `equivalent` (an extensional-equivalence claim between two functions — objective and re-checkable: a verifier re-proves it from the two bodies rather than trusting the signer). The predicate sub-tree is opaque at this layer; it must validate independently against predicate-expression.schema.json. Higher-level reasoning over claims (e.g. consequence-of, contradicts-with, supports) is the verifier's job and not part of this schema.

expr.description: A predicate-expression AST. Must validate against predicate-expression.schema.json; checked structurally only here.

predicate.description: An arbitrary predicate. Use when no more specific kind fits.

expr.description: A predicate-expression AST, exactly as in a `predicate` claim. Must validate against predicate-expression.schema.json; checked structurally only here.

trace.description: Content-address of the recorded effect trace (trace.schema.json) this claim is conditioned on. The trace is a hashless self-addressing artifact; a verifier fetches it by this address, hash-verifies it, and replays the claim's computation with every effect served from the record — the trace must be consumed exactly.

observed.description: An effectful computation's result claim, conditioned on its recorded observations: 'expr holds, given that the world responded as trace records.' Emitted by the responder whenever a fulfilment performed effects (an effectful equation is not stably re-runnable — spec/agent-loop.md §Scope). Replay-CONFIRMED means deterministic consequence of the trace, NOT that the world was as recorded — trusting the observations is a trust-model judgment about the signer. A result too large to inline rides in `expr` BY ADDRESS (`lit_blob`, predicate-expression.schema.json): the claim pins the value by the sha256 of its canonical value-expression bytes, and a verifier decides `eq(<computation>, lit_blob)` by recomputing the computation and comparing hashes — no blob travels with the message, and replay reconstructs the value for any consumer who wants it.

subject.description: Content-address of the artifact the claim is about (typically a function record).

property.description: Name of the property the subject satisfies. Resolved against the subject's own `properties[]` list (for function records).

evidence.description: Optional content-address of a proof certificate.

satisfies.description: Most common assert shape: 'subject satisfies its declared property `name`.' Sugar over a `predicate` claim of `app('satisfies', subject, property)`.

subject.description: Content-address of the artifact that has been verified.

by.description: DID of the agent that performed the verification.

proof.description: Optional content-address of the proof certificate produced.

verified.description: A meta-claim: 'this artifact has been verified by this agent (optionally with proof X).' Lets agents build attestation chains where one verifier vouches for the work of another.

subject.description: What the attestation is about: an agent DID, or the content-address of an artifact.

verb.description: The closed trust-verb vocabulary (trust-model.md open question 1, resolved): `vouches-for` is general positive trust in the subject; `trusts-claims-about` is positive trust scoped to a `domain`; `distrusts` is negative (a recipient's policy may let it override a positive path).

domain.description: The area the trust is scoped to (e.g. `rust_ingestion`). Required meaning for `trusts-claims-about`; advisory otherwise.

confidence.description: Optional strength of the attestation in [0,1]; a receiver's local policy decides how to weight it.

expires_at.description: Optional expiry; absent, the attestation SHOULD decay in influence per the receiver's local policy (trust-model.md).

attestation.description: A trust statement about an agent (or artifact): `<attester> <verb> <subject>`. The attester is the enclosing `assert` message's signer (`from`). Edges of the trust graph the reference policy engine reasons over (nl-validator evaluate-trust). Retractable via the `retract` speech act referencing the attestation's hash.

a.description: Content-address of one function. Canonical form orders `a` < `b` lexicographically (the relation is symmetric; the ordering makes duplicate claims dedupe by hash).

method.description: How the signer established the equivalence: `normal-form` = equal canonical normal forms (α-renaming + AC ordering + constant folding, solver-free); `induction` = the SMT/structural-induction equivalence prover. Advisory — a verifier recomputes regardless.

equivalent.description: An extensional-equivalence claim: `∀x. a(x) = b(x)` over the unbounded domain. OBJECTIVE and re-checkable, like a certification: `verify-claim` resolves both bodies and re-proves (equal normal forms, else the equivalence prover), so a receiver never has to take the signer's word. The attestation graph ingests a signed, verified `equivalent` claim as a symmetric `equivalent-to` edge pair — the operable form of the 'semantic equivalence vs hash equivalence' open problem: two content-addresses, one behavior, and discovery can collapse the class (spec/agent-loop.md `collapse` step). Retractable like any attestation.
